message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 8
Recent changes: Added enchantment >= 5 branch returning hardcoded 148

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll run the Refactor phase for the claim-office implementation. Let me start by reading the relevant files.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping
metrics.json
node_modules
package.json
pnpm-lock.yaml
prompt.md
run.log
src
tdd-journal.md
tsconfig.json
vitest.config.ts

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: introduced types `ScenarioResult = { premium?: number; payout?: number }` and `ScenarioOutput = { results: ScenarioResult[] }`; typed `runScenario` return as `ScenarioOutput`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — make the output contract explicit at the type level so future cycles have a shared shape for both quote (`premium`) and claim (`payout`) results
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(input): ScenarioOutput`; result entries use keys `premium` and `payout`
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: replaced `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with a `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` lookup map (`{ sword: 115, amulet: 71 }`)
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — both known item premiums are now equally enumerated rather than one being an implicit ternary fallback; also lowers APP mass (13 → 11) by removing the conditional. Map will naturally extend as upcoming staff/potion/rune/moonstone tests land
11	- Naming established: module-level `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE: Record<string, number>` as the per-item-type premium lookup
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: inlined the local `premium` binding in `runScenario` into the returned object literal (`{ premium: PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[itemType] }`); also added `staff: 93` to the lookup map (this was the Green change)
15	- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the named binding was redundant because the object key `premium:` already self-documents the value; mass −1. Kept `itemType` binding because `steps[0].items[0].type` is about to be reused once surcharges/discounts land
16	- Naming established: —
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): Green only added `potion: 49` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Tried destructuring `steps[0]`/`items[0]` into `firstStep`/`firstItem` bindings but reverted — added two bindings with no clarity gain over the direct `steps[0].items[0].type` access. Extracting the implicit `base * 1.1 + 5` formula behind the cached premiums would be premature: upcoming loyalty-discount tests will replace the `* 1.1` first-contract surcharge, and only then will the fee/surcharge split become observable. Tightening `Step.op: string` to a literal union would speculate on the claim-op shape. Function stays at ~8 mass, fully justified by 4 passing tests.
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): Green added `rune: 33` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Rune is the first test where rounding becomes implicit (25 * 1.1 = 27.5 → ceil → 28, then +5 = 33), giving the first weak signal that a `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` formula extraction with a `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` map is coming. Holding the extraction because: (a) `Math.ceil`, `Math.round`, and `Math.trunc+1` all satisfy this lone non-integer case — no test yet forces ceil specifically; (b) the dedicated "round UP in MHPCO's favor" todo (staff + loyalty + first = 76G from 70.4) will force ceil unambiguously and will land alongside the loyalty discount, at which point extracting base+formula+rounding together is one coherent change instead of two speculative ones. Naming/binding/element review unchanged from cycle 4.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const COMPONENT_PREMIUM = 33` and referenced it from both `rune` and `moonstone` entries in `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`
26	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) + partial Rule 3 (literal duplication) — moonstone is the second 33G entry, and the spec calls rune/moonstone "components" sharing base 25G. Naming the shared value documents the category rather than leaving it a coincidence of two equal literals. Anticipates upcoming component-block tests (lines 96-100) that will distinguish component pricing further. Module-level mass +1; accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` + `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` — cycle 5 reasoning unchanged (ceil not yet forced; loyalty/fee split not yet observable).
27	- Naming established: module-level `COMPONENT_PREMIUM: number` as the shared per-unit premium for component items (rune, moonstone, …)
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- No refactoring (justified): Green added a literal `if (item.cursed) return { results: [{ premium: 170 }] }` branch and a small `item = steps[0].items[0]` binding (reused twice). Evaluated three Rule-2 candidates and rejected all: (a) replacing `170` with `100 * 1.5 * 1.1 + 5` matches the spec phrasing but adds ~+9 mass for a one-off intermediate-value reveal that will be obsoleted next cycle by the highly-enchanted (148G) and loyalty (93G) sword tests, which together force a `Math.ceil(base * surcharges * 1.1) + 5` extraction with named multipliers — doing it now would be the speculative two-step path cycle 5 warned against; (b) introducing `CURSED_SWORD_PREMIUM = 170` just relabels the magic number without tying it to the sword base of 115 already in the lookup — no Rule 3 win since `cursed` is per-item not per-type so it can't fold into `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`; (c) replacing `170` with `Math.ceil(100 * 1.5 * 1.1) + 5` is the same speculation as (a) plus a ceil that this integer test doesn't force. The cursed branch is intentionally parked as a hardcoded-return placeholder, mirroring how per-type premiums sat as bare numbers in cycles 1-2 before `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` emerged from accumulated signal. Extraction will happen when the surcharge family (highly-enchanted, loyalty, first-contract, stacking, round-up-staff) has landed enough tests to drive one coherent `applyMultipliers` + base-lookup + ceil refactor. Naming on `item`, `runScenario`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `COMPONENT_PREMIUM` all still accurate.
31

content.text: Good context. I now understand cycle 8 — the latest Green added a parallel hardcoded branch for enchantment >= 5 returning 148. Let me check tests pass and analyze.

 ✓ src/claim-office.spec.ts  (38 tests | 30 skipped) 3ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed | 30 todo (38)
   Start at  20:13:24
   Duration  157ms (transform 30ms, setup 0ms, collect 28ms, tests 3ms, environment 0ms, prepare 42ms)

content.text:
All 8 tests pass. Now let me evaluate refactoring.

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation**
- `runScenario` — still accurate, processes a scenario input
- `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` — still accurate
- `COMPONENT_PREMIUM` — still accurate
- `item` — clear local binding

All names still reveal intent.

**Step 2: Calculate Current APP Mass** (function body, lines 19-29)

```
const { steps } = input as Input;          // 1 binding + 1 binding (input) = 2; 1 invocation (cast)? casts are free
const item = steps[0].items[0];            // 1 binding + 2 constants (0,0) + 2 invocations (index) ≈ 5
if (item.cursed) { return ... 170 }        // 1 conditional (4) + 1 invocation (.cursed) (2) + 1 constant (170) = 7
if ((item.enchantment ?? 0) >= 5) { 148 }  // 1 conditional (4) + 1 invocation (.enchantment) (2) + 2 constants (0,5) (2) + 1 invocation (??) (2) + 1 invocation (>=) (2) + 1 constant (148) = 13
return { premium: PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[item.type] }  // 1 invocation (lookup) + 1 invocation (.type) + 1 constant = ~4
```

Approximate body mass: ~31 (rough).

**Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules**

- **Rule 1**: All tests pass ✅
- **Rule 2 (intent)**: The two surcharge branches share a "compute premium for a sword variant" pattern. Both return hardcoded `170` and `148` — the relationship to the base sword price (115G) and to surcharges (1.5x cursed, 1.3x highly enchanted) is invisible.
- **Rule 3 (duplication)**: Conceptual duplication — two `if return hardcoded` branches mirror each other (cursed branch and highly-enchanted branch). Both are placeholders. This is the second occurrence of the pattern.
- **Rule 4 (fewest elements)**: Function has grown to 3 branches.

**Decision**: Cycle 7's documented justification explicitly anticipated this exact cycle and parked extraction until the surcharge family had enough signal. We now have two parallel placeholder branches. The cycle-7 plan said extraction should happen "when the surcharge family has landed enough tests to drive one coherent `applyMultipliers` + base-lookup + ceil refactor."

However, only TWO surcharges are present, both as standalone single-item flat returns. Loyalty discount, first-contract surcharge, ceil rounding, and stacking are all still unforced. Extracting `applyMultipliers` now would require speculating on:
- whether the formula is `Math.ceil(base * multipliers * 1.1) + 5` or some other shape (no rounding test yet forces ceil; loyalty/first-contract not yet observable)
- whether cursed and highly-enchanted stack multiplicatively (todo on line 127 confirms, but that test isn't active)
- how the base 115G decomposes into 100G + 5G fee + 1.1 first-contract

All the active tests are still single-multiplier single-item integer cases. The two hardcoded returns (170, 148) are still parallel and both will be replaced together once stacking + loyalty + ceil land.

That said, there IS a small Rule 2/Rule 3 win available right now that doesn't speculate: the two surcharge branches both check a property on `item` and return a hardcoded single-item-single-premium result. The shape `return { results: [{ premium: X }] }` is now repeated 3 times. Let me consider extracting a small helper.

Candidate refactor: extract `function singlePremium(amount: number): ScenarioOutput { return { results: [{ premium: amount }] }; }`.

Mass-wise: removes 3 nested object literal constructions (each ~3 mass: 2 invocations for object construction + array construction) and replaces with 3 invocations (3 * 2 = 6) + 1 helper definition (~6). Roughly neutral on mass, but adds an extra binding/function. Rule 2: the helper name `singlePremium` would reveal that all three branches are returning a single-item single-result with just a premium. Rule 4: adds an element.

Honestly this is borderline. The shape `{ results: [{ premium: X }] }` is the literal shape of the output contract — naming it `singlePremium` doesn't add much that isn't already revealed by the types. And the next test (enchantment=4 boundary at 115G) will likely just merge into existing logic, while the loyalty/first-contract tests will force formula extraction that restructures all three returns simultaneously.

Let me also consider: a tiny Rule 2 win on the `>= 5` conditional. The literal `5` is the "highly enchanted threshold" per the spec. Extracting `const HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN = 5` would name the magic number and document the threshold. Mass impact: +1 binding at module scope, but the literal `5` becomes a named binding reference (no mass change — both count as 1). Net mass: +1. Rule 2 win: small but real and non-speculative — the spec uses the phrase "highly enchanted" and the boundary test at enchantment=4 is the next todo, which makes the threshold semantically load-bearing.

This mirrors exactly what cycle 6 did with `COMPONENT_PREMIUM`: name a magic number that the spec already names. The pending boundary test (line 118) makes this name semantically meaningful — there's about to be an enchantment=4 test verifying the boundary, and a named threshold documents which side of `5` is "highly enchanted."

I'll do that extraction.

**Step 4: Implement Refactoring**

content.text:
Tests still pass. The task tracker is owned by the orchestrator — I'll leave it. Now let me append to architecture-notes.

**Step 5: Recalculate Mass**

Net mass change: +1 (added module-level binding `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`), but the literal `5` becomes a named reference (both score 1). The function body mass is unchanged; module mass +1.

**Step 6: Document**

input.new_string:
## After cycle 8
- Change: extracted `const HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN = 5` at module scope and replaced the literal `5` in the `item.enchantment >= 5` guard with the named binding
- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the spec uses the phrase "highly enchanted" and the very next test (line 118, enchantment=4 boundary => stays at 115G) makes this threshold semantically load-bearing. Naming the magic number documents which side of the boundary is "highly enchanted" before that test forces the discussion. Mirrors cycle 6's `COMPONENT_PREMIUM` extraction — name the threshold the spec already names. Module mass +1 (new binding); function body mass unchanged (literal 5 → binding ref, both score 1). Accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting the formula/`applyMultipliers` helper or replacing the hardcoded 170/148 with `Math.ceil(base * multipliers * 1.1) + 5`: cycle 7's reasoning still holds — only two surcharges are active and both are single-multiplier single-item integer cases; loyalty discount, first-contract surcharge, ceil rounding (round-up-staff test), and stacking (cursed+enchanted=220G) are all still unforced. The two parallel hardcoded branches (170, 148) will be replaced together by the same coherent refactor once the surcharge family lands enough signal.
- Naming established: module-level `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN: number` as the inclusive enchantment threshold at which an item counts as "highly enchanted" for quote surcharging (and, anticipated, claim eligibility)

## After cycle 7
- No refactoring (justified):

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Naming Evaluation**: All existing names (`runScenario`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `COMPONENT_PREMIUM`, `item`) still accurately reveal intent — no renames needed.

**Refactoring Applied**: Extracted `const HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN = 5` at module scope and replaced the literal `5` in the `item.enchantment >= 5` guard with the named binding (Rule 2 — reveals intent). The spec uses the phrase "highly enchanted" and the very next test (enchantment=4 boundary) makes this threshold semantically load-bearing.

**Mass Change**: Module mass +1 (new binding); function body unchanged (literal → binding ref, both score 1). Accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP.
